definition: Open to more than one interpretation; deliberately ambiguous or evasive.
usage_notes: Often implies intentional vagueness to avoid commitment
Stronger than 'ambiguous' — carries connotation of evasiveness
Common in political, legal, and diplomatic language

etymology.origin_summary: From Latin 'aequivocus' — of equal voice. 'aequi-' (equal) + 'vocare' (to call). Having two equally valid meanings.

examples: The senator gave an equivocal answer to avoid alienating either side.
The test results were equivocal and required further analysis.

confusions.difference: 'Ambiguous' can be accidental; 'equivocal' implies deliberate evasiveness or equal interpretations.
'Evasive' means deliberately avoiding an answer; 'equivocal' means giving an answer with multiple readings.
